words: SAM SCHRAGER: This moving to Montana, why did you move there in the first place?
RANNIE VINE:   Well, they advertised that part of the country at that time, like
they do everything else and homesteading and one thing and another,
so my husband and- several other men with families that just took the
notion they wanted to go on out there and homestead. 320 acres of
land sounded big because we came from Wisconsin, Wisconsin isn't too
big of a state and it was all dairying and my husband didn't want
nothing to do with a cow, he said, or a calf, too, how in the world
a person s going to make a living stripping a,cow every night and
morning. (Chuckles) So, he didn't like it, so they went on out
there and we done alright.

speaker: SS
words: Had he been dairying in Wisconsin?

speaker: SS
words: VINE:   Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Were the people that went on out with you neighbors of yours?

speaker: SS
words: VINE:   Yes, and a lot of his relatives. The father and mother came out with

speaker: SS
words: us and one of his brothers and my brother came out there with us,

speaker: SS
words: but he went back. He said, "None of this for me." He went back

speaker: SS
words: to farming there in Wisconsin, says, "That's good enough for me."

speaker: SS
words: Where in Wisconsin?

speaker: SS
words: VINE:   Have you ever been down in there?

speaker: SS
words: A little bit, I've driven through it more.

speaker: SS
words: VINE:   Do you know where Chippewa Falls is?

speaker: SS
words: I think, yeah, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: VINE:   Oh, I don't know how far east of St. Paul that would be, probably

speaker: SS
words: 150 miles or something like that. We were just about in the central

speaker: SS
words: part of Wisconsin. Small town, they called Greenwood. That was an old

speaker: SS
words: logging town, too.

speaker: SS
words: Greemood?

speaker: SS
words: Uh-huh. Greenwood was an old logging town. And when I was a child

speaker: SS
words: I've heard 'em tell about- well, when they pull the sawmill outit

speaker: SS
words: would be the last of the lumbering and woodswork around Greenwood.

speaker: SS
words: They still are hauling in and they got little patches- let's see what

speaker: SS
words: do they call them now- they make boxes for up in the torthem part of

speaker: SS
words: Wisconsin for fishing boxes and stuff like that to put the fish in.

speaker: SS
words: This decision to go to Montana; do you think that they- do you think

speaker: SS
words: that your husband and others, that you all felt that you could betterdo

speaker: SS
words: a lot better out in Montana than in Wisconsin?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, my husband did, one reason he didn't like that dairying

speaker: SS
words: and he was born in a lumber camp in Wisconsin and of course, that's

speaker: SS
words: what he liked, and when he got out into Montana, he done alright,

speaker: SS
words: although he wasn't a very good farmer, but then when the crops began to

speaker: SS
words: fail, why, out on the coast, out in the timber again, out, come

speaker: SS
words: out here.

speaker: SS
words: Would you tell me a little bit about what it was like around Fort

speaker: SS
words: Peck when you got there? Was it at all developed already?

speaker: SS
words: Fort Peck, of course- Let's see, on the north side of the Missouri River, and this

speaker: SS
words: homesteading that they had agoing was south

speaker: SS
words: of the Missouri so we had to cross a ferry to get on the south side

speaker: SS
words: of the river. And in 1910 there wasn't anybody- there were just a

speaker: SS
words: few; very few people that had started to homestead, but during 1910

speaker: SS
words: and '11, why, there was cabins sprung up on most every 320 acres all

speaker: SS
words: around there. So there was people from North Dakota that lived there

speaker: SS
words: and done okay, I guess, I don't know, anyway they homesteaded. We

speaker: SS
words: homesteaded, I think it was twenty miles south of the Missouri River

speaker: SS
words: went right- Popl^lIP that town where we come that far by train and

speaker: SS
words: there, that's right on the bank of the Missouri there and we homesteaded

speaker: SS
words: about twenty miles south of there and then there was another

speaker: SS
words: twenty miles further and there was any amount of people who

speaker: SS
words: moved in there. And the second year we were there, 1911, why there

speaker: SS
words: was a- I don't know what they were, I think they were some foreign

speaker: SS
words: ers, anyway foreigners of some kind, either Hollanders or Germans

speaker: SS
words: or something like that had farmed in North Dakota, then they took

speaker: SS
words: up these homesteads, the Old Man did, his sons they took

speaker: SS
words: up land- an awful lot of it up there. And they brought in their

speaker: SS
words: equipment from North Dakota, you see, great, big, old tractors and

speaker: SS
words: plows and what you have and so when they got as far as our place,

speaker: SS
words: why, this old fella says, he says, "I'd like to plow that field over

speaker: SS
words: there for ya-. "I think it was something like 100 acres or so or some

speaker: SS
words: thing like that over there, and my husband says, he says, "I haven't

speaker: SS
words: got the money now," he says, "to pay for." He says, "I ain't asking

speaker: SS
words: you for any money." He says, "Let me plow that," and he says, "If

speaker: SS
words: you can, put it into flax." And he says, "I'll wait for my pay.

speaker: SS
words: and got lots of builtins to do then that

speaker: SS
words: boys can do that. So that s the way it happened. I never saw

speaker: SS
words: such beautiful flax in my life. Grow up about this high, and just

speaker: SS
words: as level, and just wonderful. Made all kinds of money out of that

speaker: SS
words: flax; Atwo straight years, and that kicjn of put us on our feet.

speaker: SS
words: Did you build a homestead cabin on the place?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, we shipped up two railroad cars full of oh, lumber, and every

speaker: SS
words: thing that we needed from Wisconsin. We had quite a nice little

speaker: SS
words: house on our . And people them days were so different from

speaker: SS
words: what they are now; now there's a carpenter that lived down close

speaker: SS
words: to the Missouri, and his land would be flooded every year, you know,

speaker: SS
words: the ice would jam up and then it would flood his land so he couldn't

speaker: SS
words: do much of anything, so he done carpenter work for people that wanted

speaker: SS
words: to build . So he come to my husband and we had lots of lum-

speaker: SS
words: ber on hand and he says, "Let me build you a barn, a good, big one

speaker: SS
words: with a good hayloft." And we had some maple flooring with us- stuff

speaker: SS
words: about this wide, you know, and he said, "Put that in the loft," he

speaker: SS
words: says, "and use that for a dance hall for a Tvrhile." He says, "I'll

speaker: SS
words: bet you'll have that paid for in just no time." So that's the way

speaker: SS
words: it happened. And we shipped the piano out with us, and we had that,

speaker: SS
words: so when he had the barn finished, why, he says, "Now," he says, "Let's

speaker: SS
words: give a dance to advertise a little bit." So they did. I never seen

speaker: SS
words: so many people in my life as there was come to that.

speaker: SS
words: come for - from Miles City and all of those places. We didn't have

speaker: SS
words: foo d enough to feed 'em or anything. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Did you have the dances there very often after that?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, big ones, for Red Cross and one thing and another. We made

speaker: SS
words: an awful lot of money for the Red Cross. We didn't put any hay in

speaker: SS
words: there for a long time. I don't know as we ever put hay in it. You

speaker: SS
words: know that prairie hay, you could stack that.

speaker: SS
words: When you had a dance there would it be often -just a community dance,

speaker: SS
words: just for local families?

speaker: SS
words: Well, once in a while we'd have it just for the neighborhood or some

speaker: SS
words: thing like that, or try to. I remember one time the neighbors said

speaker: SS
words: let's have a little party and dance Saturday night. And I said',' Okay."

speaker: SS
words: And we did, and first thing we knew, why, you look out and here was

speaker: SS
words: horses and cowboys and women and kids all over the place!(Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: You couldn't hardly keep 'em away?

speaker: SS
words: Ya, that was advertised, oh, all around

speaker: SS
words: Lot of work about it. I enjoyed it. And then there was

speaker: SS
words: quite a few people from Chicago that come out there and homesteaded.

speaker: SS
words: And some of 'em had been in the baseball leagues in Chicago, and

speaker: SS
words: started a baseball outfit. And that was every Sunday and every Sun-

speaker: SS
words: day. I might just as well bake up twenty loaves of bread and cook

speaker: SS
words: up a ham or two or something else and feed that bunch every Sunday.

speaker: SS
words: And enjoyed every minute of it!

speaker: SS
words: Where did they play at? Close to the home?

speaker: SS
words: Yes. Here is our homestead, here's my father-in-law's homestead,

speaker: SS
words: here's my sister-in-law's homestead and here is Hubert's- that's

speaker: SS
words: another fella that come.Greenwood with us. So, we had our buildings

speaker: SS
words: right close here, and this sister-in-law- she wasn't married yet to

speaker: SS
words: my brother-in-law, but anyway, they were going to be- but she come

speaker: SS
words: out there and homesteaded, and-

speaker: SS
words: She homesteaded?

speaker: SS
words: She homesteaded- teacher, yeah- and my two oldest girls and three

speaker: SS
words: other children, I guess, she taught them. She was out here last sum

speaker: SS
words: mer.

speaker: SS
words: Really? Herself?

speaker: SS
words: Well, she came from Wisconsin with us and she just Aofc that urge

speaker: SS
words: that she wanted to come West. She taught school in our neighbor

speaker: SS
words: hood in Wisconsin, and that's how she got acquainted with my brotherin-

speaker: SS
words: law. And she wanted to come out and she came out with us. And

speaker: SS
words: she had her homestead- let's see- I got her whole history here. The

speaker: SS
words: whole business.

speaker: SS
words: Did she write it down?

speaker: SS
words: She wrote it. I just wonder where I got that. blank space—

speaker: SS
words: A lot of hard work, too?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, yeah, but everybody worked hard thef% We didn't think anything

speaker: SS
words: about it. Different than it is now. Why, that work had to be done^

speaker: SS
words: you might just as well get in an do it- forget about it.

speaker: SS
words: What was the hard work, when you think about it now?

speaker: SS
words: Well, as far as housekeeping- we always had so many hired men- not

speaker: SS
words: many, I shouldn't say that- about eight or ten all the time. And

speaker: SS
words: we had to haul all our water a mile and a half; drinking water,

speaker: SS
words: wash water, bath water and the whole works. And then the water was

speaker: SS
words: so hard you had to break it with lye. And the winters are so severe

speaker: SS
words: back there. Sixty below. The people didn't think anything about

speaker: SS
words: it, we didn't know any better, I guess. You know, a lot of peo-

speaker: SS
words: pie now, you start to tell em and of course, the younger

speaker: SS
words: people's not interested in it, and it can't be so, that's all there

speaker: SS
words: is to it. (Laughter) But anyway, we never thought about that. You

speaker: SS
words: know, people this day and feel like they got to be right close

speaker: SS
words: to a doctor all the time. Well, I raised my family out there on the

speaker: SS
words: hill- or in Montana, I shouldn't say hills, because it wasn't, it

speaker: SS
words: was flat as could be, but we never thought about a doctor. Never

speaker: SS
words: gave it a thought. We got along just fine. And it was during thatin

speaker: SS
words: 1918, that flu-

speaker: SS
words: You thinking of that flu?

speaker: SS
words: When that flu was so bad.

speaker: SS
words: What about that?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, that was terrible. Any amount of people that died right out

speaker: SS
words: there on the prairie with that. Not any more than they did

speaker: SS
words: in town, because there wasn't anything much that a doctor could do

speaker: SS
words: for it. Mostly the people that died was pregnant mothers. Their

speaker: SS
words: temperature'd run so high that it killed 'em that way. It struck

speaker: SS
words: a neighborhood just north of us a little ways, and those people came

speaker: SS
words: from Canada and homesteaded in there. French people. They were hit

speaker: SS
words: awful hard, there was several of them that died. But anyway, we

speaker: SS
words: helped one another and we got along just swell.

speaker: SS
words: Was there any treatment- was there any way of treating it, that you

speaker: SS
words: used out there?

speaker: SS
words: VINE:   Well, they didn't have anything much to treat it with. And the doc

speaker: SS
words: tor came up once or twice- and I happened- they all called on me for

speaker: SS
words: some reason or other, I don't know why. If the children was sick

speaker: SS
words: or anything they^had to have me and I didn't know anything about

speaker: SS
words: those things much. But, anyway he'd give 'em shots, you know, -md

speaker: SS
words: then give me orders what to do. Temperature gets up so high, why,

speaker: SS
words: give 'em a shot, and I never did, I was supposed to, but I didn't kids

speaker: SS
words: lived anyway! (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: In general, what of remedies did you use there? I mean, con

speaker: SS
words: sidering you didn't have a doctor

speaker: SS
words: He didn't give us anything!

speaker: SS
words: I don't mean just for the flu, but there was a lot of sickness of

speaker: SS
words: different sorts, I know in the pioneering. I mean, what would you

speaker: SS
words: do when kids were sick or parents were sick? Were there any plants

speaker: SS
words: or any medicines that were commonly used?

speaker: SS
words: VINE:   Not that I know of. I took care of a lot of them when they first

speaker: SS
words: started to come down, and the trouble was, try to break the temperature.

speaker: SS
words: We had no aspirins, nothing like that them days; nothing.

speaker: SS
words: Khere was an old woman, I think she's part Indian. She says, "You

speaker: SS
words: know," she says, "you've to get 'em to perspire." And she says,

speaker: SS
words: "The way that we used to do it," she says, "is to go to the corncrib

speaker: SS
words: and get them to count the corn and put 'em in the boiler and put 'em

speaker: SS
words: on to boiling and get 'em good and hot; wrap 'em in Turkish towels

speaker: SS
words: and then lay 'em next to the patients." And that's what I did, and

speaker: SS
words: I had more luck with that than anything*

speaker: SS
words: They'd just break out wringing wet and that's when they kind of re

speaker: SS
words: laxed.

speaker: SS
words: What about tuberculosis? Was there any of that consumption?

speaker: SS
words: You want me to tell you one of them stories, too?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: I had that, when I lived on the ranch. And, of course, it was-Awe

speaker: SS
words: called it them days- slow consumption. And you just kept goin'

speaker: SS
words: down hill and you kept workin' and goin' downhill and you'd lose

speaker: SS
words: your appetite and you couldn't eat and you hatl this work to

speaker: SS
words: do and you had to keep up. So, my folks lived, a lot of 'em in

speaker: SS
words: North Dakota, and so they said that I better come on down there.

speaker: SS
words: And I thought , well, I can't do-^ I couldn't believe that I had it

speaker: SS
words: in the first place. I went to seven different doctors before I'd

speaker: SS
words: really make up my mind that they knew what they were talkin' about.

speaker: SS
words: But finally I had to go down there. And their medical doctor was

speaker: SS
words: in Bottineau, North Dakota and their surgeon was in Rugby, so of

speaker: SS
words: course they took me on up to Bottineau and he wanted to send me to

speaker: SS
words: a sanitarium up at Dunseith, up near Canada up in there. I said, "No,

speaker: SS
words: not me." And my sister had a large family, she said, "Well," she

speaker: SS
words: says, "We'll, just put her in a room," and she says, "one of the

speaker: SS
words: girls'11 just take over and take care of her, and that's it." And

speaker: SS
words: so I laid there for a couple or three months and they'd take me to

speaker: SS
words: Bottineau once in a while for a check up and finally their doctor at

speaker: SS
words: Bottineau said, "Well," he said, "she won't last only about three

speaker: SS
words: more days, that's all there is to it." He said, "If the folks want

speaker: SS
words: to see her, they better come on down." So coming home from Bottineau

speaker: SS
words: they lived up on a large farm close to Sourls,North Dakota. Well,

speaker: SS
words: anyway, coming home here they set and bawled and bawled and bawled

speaker: SS
words: and I says, "What's the matter?" I says, "I know what he said," I

speaker: SS
words: says, "I'm doomed, and I can't be here much longer." But, I says,

speaker: SS
words: "What's the difference? Got to go sometime." And, then they really

speaker: SS
words: did bawl. Well,anyway when they got home they sent for the folks.

speaker: SS
words: And they were to come the next day, they did. The next mor

speaker: SS
words: ning then I was so weak and had been- they had to carry me into the

speaker: SS
words: car and out of the car and into the doctor's office and all of that

speaker: SS
words: sort of stuff. But anyway, my brother-in-law used to come^up to

speaker: SS
words: my room about three o'clock in the morning- it was in the month of

speaker: SS
words: June- nice and light, you know. And they'd send him up there first

speaker: SS
words: for fear that probably I was gone or something like that, so this

speaker: SS
words: morning he came on up and looked at me and I just smiled; I couldn't

speaker: SS
words: talk or anything, I'd cough my head off and I couldn't hardly raise

speaker: SS
words: my hand, so he went back down, an then my niece came up that was ta

speaker: SS
words: king care of me and gave me my bath- I guess she did, I don't remember

speaker: SS
words: that, but anyway she fed me and that and I was so weak I couldnt

speaker: SS
words: £nd.I grabbed ahold of a piece of her dress and I held onto to it

speaker: SS
words: and I says, "I want to get up." And I scared her to death. But be

speaker: SS
words: tween the time that my brother-in-law was there my niece I went thru

speaker: SS
words: something but I don't know what it was- I can't tell ya. I had chills

speaker: SS
words: and sweats and everything else. I never could explain it; there's

speaker: SS
words: no way to explain it. And came to me and says, "Get up and

speaker: SS
words: get out of that bed and go home!" And so when she came up, I just had

speaker: SS
words: to follow this instruction- and she says, "You can't get up!" And I

speaker: SS
words: says,- "You can't even set up to get dressed." And I says, - and

speaker: SS
words: there was an old-fashioned chairw- and it took to tell her

speaker: SS
words: this- "Tip the bottom side up," I says, "and slide the back of the

speaker: SS
words: chair in under my back and raise me up gradually, til you get my

speaker: SS
words: clothes on." So, she started, then she went down stairs and, "Oh,

speaker: SS
words: she says, "Auntie's just about gone." She said. And then they

speaker: SS
words: cided down there, they'd do what they could for me anyway,

speaker: SS
words: so. She came back up and started to dress me and at three o'

speaker: SS
words: clock that afternoon I was up and dressed and I've never coughed and

speaker: SS
words: I've never been in bed with it since! Had the strength to walk down

speaker: SS
words: them stairs and everything at three o'clock in the afternoon. Now,

speaker: SS
words: that's unbelievable, isn't it?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: That's a miracle.

speaker: SS
words: Incredible!

speaker: SS
words: That's a miracle! But that's what happened. And in three days af

speaker: SS
words: ter that I went home, and here I'd been foolin' around with it for

speaker: SS
words: several years, and I was okay.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know that voice that you heard speaking to you, is that all

speaker: SS
words: that it said?

speaker: SS
words: Huh?

speaker: SS
words: Is that all that the voice said, just to get up and get your clothes

speaker: SS
words: on?

speaker: SS
words: All of it? Yeah. Well, this voice says, "Get up and get ready and

speaker: SS
words: go home." Something like that. "And live, because you've got a lot

speaker: SS
words: to live for." And that was it. But I had to do it, I had to follow

speaker: SS
words: those instructions. Just had to!

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the chills and all that that had happened to you

speaker: SS
words: then was like some kind of a turning point?

speaker: SS
words: Sure it was. Whatever this was- really it turned things. There is

speaker: SS
words: a higher power, I'll tell you than doctors. And then my husband came

speaker: SS
words: and my daughter and^then in about two years, I guess after that I

speaker: SS
words: went down there to visit again, and my sister says, "Let's go on down

speaker: SS
words: Jbo Bottineau and see Dr. Mc Kay." And I says, "Okay." Well, they

speaker: SS
words: took me in there and when it come my time for him to talk to me,

speaker: SS
words: why, I says, "I guess you don't remember me, do you?" And, he says,

speaker: SS
words: "No." "Well," I says, "I'm Mrs. Johnson's sister that was here so

speaker: SS
words: sick a couple of years ago." And he began to back up, and back up

speaker: SS
words: and he looked at me andAsays, "Miracles will happen." And he just

speaker: SS
words: went and set down.

speaker: SS
words: How old were you at that time when that happened, more or less?

speaker: SS
words: Well, I think I wasjc I don't remember- I don't keep track of those

speaker: SS
words: things.

speaker: SS
words: Well, during the time you were in Montana-

speaker: SS
words: Three years. So it was about forty some years ago- fifty

speaker: SS
words: some years ago.

speaker: SS
words: Well, if you had died, I suppose it would have been a real awful

speaker: SS
words: blow to the family.

speaker: SS
words: Well, they were kind of prepared for it, because I'd been sick so

speaker: SS
words: long.

speaker: SS
words: When you were sick like that and you didn't really want to believe

speaker: SS
words: it, did it really impair what you could do, did it get in your way

speaker: SS
words: a lot, did it stop you from doing the work that you wanted to do?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, yes, I didn't have the strength. I didn't have the strength

speaker: SS
words: to go ahead and do anything, much. But, when I got home it didn't

speaker: SS
words: take me long to get back.

speaker: SS
words: One or two that I remember of. I think in this book that I gave you

speaker: SS
words: that Alida, she'd tell about all.that stuff in that.

speaker: SS
words: How far around did the kids come to the school?

speaker: SS
words: Huh?

speaker: SS
words: How far around did the kids come to the school that she taught?

speaker: SS
words: Well, she taught right in our homestead shack. And that was right

speaker: SS
words: close to our place, you know, so the kids didn't have very far to

speaker: SS
words: go, until more settlers settled there and then they built a schoolhouse

speaker: SS
words: about a mile and a half from our place. Kind of in the center

speaker: SS
words: of the whole neighborhood, you see. I don't know, I never did have

speaker: SS
words: a picture of that schoolhouse, but she taught there.

speaker: SS
words: How serious was the water shortage?

speaker: SS
words: Well, it wasn't such an awful shortage, but it was so hard; so many

speaker: SS
words: minerals and oil and that that you couldn't use it. We had three

speaker: SS
words: wells on our place but we couldn't use the water for any of it orlv

speaker: SS
words: Kto water the stock. And now, the one that wrote that book, they re

speaker: SS
words: still hauling water from the well that we did a mile and a half away.

speaker: SS
words: They built a pond for to water their stock and for things like that

speaker: SS
words: but for their use in the house, cooking and that, they have to haul

speaker: SS
words: their water. Just about the only well around the country that the

speaker: SS
words: water is fit to use.

speaker: SS
words: In your visiting time, I mean the time that you had to get together,

speaker: SS
words: was it mostly with your kin? Mostly with your relatives, that you

speaker: SS
words: would spend your time on that homestead, or was there a lot of other

speaker: SS
words: neighbors, too, that you would get together with?

speaker: SS
words: Well, we had- and I guess they still do out there- had ft Ladies'

speaker: SS
words: Aid and all that sort of stuff. And I don't know- yeah the French,/^

speaker: SS
